title: The Sixth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## Sermon Date: July 21, 2019

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Sixth Sunday after Pentecost in Ordinary Time (also called the Ninth Sunday in some traditions). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would have been 2 Kings 4:42-44, Psalm 145:10-18, Ephesians 3:14-21, and John 6:1-21, featuring the first part of the Bread of Life discourse and the feeding of the five thousand. The church was in the long green season of Ordinary Time, approximately 10 weeks into the post-Pentecost period.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was navigating the aftermath of the 2018 General Convention (Austin), which had addressed issues of gun violence, racial justice, and Anglican Communion relations. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Robert Imhoff (who served until 2021), was focused on diocesan ministry and reconciliation efforts. No major bishop elections or exceptional controversies were occurring in the Rocky Mountain region at this specific moment, though national discussions about LGBTQ+ inclusion and women's leadership continued as baseline Episcopal Church conversations.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing mid-summer heat typical for late July. The city's downtown and Capitol Hill neighborhoods were busy with summer tourism and entertainment. Colorado was in the midst of Democratic primary election season (2020 cycle beginning), with several state and local political campaigns ramping up. No major weather disasters or immediate civic crises were dominating local headlines in the immediate week prior to July 21.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Trump administration was dealing with immigration crises at the southern border, with ongoing media coverage of detention facilities and family separations (ongoing since 2018, intensified in 2019). The 2020 presidential campaign was actively underway with Democratic primary debates held the previous week (July 16-18 in Detroit). Mueller Report discussions and potential impeachment proceedings remained in political conversation. The economy was strong with low unemployment, though trade war tensions with China continued.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Hong Kong protests, which began in March 2019 over an extradition bill, had escalated dramatically in early July with major clashes between demonstrators and police; by mid-July, the movement was intensifying. Britain's Conservative Party was in the midst of leadership elections to replace Theresa May, with Boris Johnson emerging as the likely winner (announced July 23, two days after this sermon). The Brazilian Amazon fires were beginning their seasonal surge, though major international media attention would come later in August.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American society was deeply polarized along partisan lines, with social media amplifying divisions. The mood was tense regarding immigration, racial justice, and political accountability. In popular culture, "The Lion King" had just been released (July 19), dominating entertainment discussion. The Marvel Cinematic Universe remained a cultural touchstone with "Avengers: Endgame" having been the dominant film earlier in the year. Sports fans were following MLB and the run-up to the 2020 Olympics (scheduled for Tokyo). There was a general sense of social turbulence mixed with summer leisure.
